content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
Patti Walker 
Mayor, City of Fillmore 
Central Park Plaza 
250 Central A venue 
Fillmore, California 93015-1907 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-11-007 
Dear Ms. Walker: 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
April 18, 2011 
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that our advice is based solely 
on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other 
conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 
1090. You are writing on behalf of Councilmember Brian Sipes. 
QUESTIONS 
May Councilmember Sipes participate in decisions involving: 
1. the expansion or elimination of or the setting of eligibility criteria for the city's First 
Time Buyer Program (the "Program") if clients of a business in which he is a partner apply for 
the Program, or 
2. the salary, performance evaluations or continued employment of the city manager who 
leases property from a partnership in which Councilmember Sipes is a partner? 
CONCLUSIONS 
l. Councilmember Sipes may participate in a decision regarding the Program unless it 
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect (i) his company's annual gross revenues or 
assets by $20,000 or expenses by $5,000, or (ii) the income, investments, or other assets or 
liabilities (other than real property) of any of the company's client's by $1,000. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9 IO 14. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 IO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-11-007 
Page No. 2 
2. Councilmember Sipes may not participate in a decision regarding the city manager's 
employment because it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on the city manager's income. 
FACTS 
Brian Sipes was recently elected to the city council for the City of Fillmore. He, together 
with his mother, is a partner in a local mortgage lending company (the "Mortgage Company"). 
Prior to his election, Councilmember Sipes and his Mortgage Company have arranged loans for 
home buyers. Some of his clients have applied for the city's Program. The Program provides a 
loan of up to $50,000 for qualified first time home buyers to be used for a down payment and 
closing costs. A borrower's application is reviewed by staff and then presented to the Finance 
Committee for approval. The Finance Committee has two members. Councilmember Sipes is 
not a member of the Finance Committee and has no authority to appoint members to the Finance 
Committee. 
Once a client of the Mortgage Company has submitted an application, the Mortgage 
Company follows up on the processing of the application. The Mortgage Company does not fill 
out the application for its clients. The Mortgage Company does not receive any payment from 
proceeds of the loan. 
Applications for the Program do not come before the city council. However, the city 
council has the power to vote to expand or eliminate the Program or to set eligibility criteria for 
the Program. There are no current plans to do so. 
Councilmember Sipes is also a partner in a partnership with three other individuals that 
own properties within the city limits (the "Real Estate Partnership.") One of the properties is 
leased to the Fillmore city manager. Title to the property is held in the names of all four 
partners. Councilmember Sipes is not a managing partner of the Real Estate Partnership. He has 
no involvement in the leasing of the properties, nor was he involved in the lease agreement 
between the Real Estate Partnership and the city manager. The lease agreement was entered into 
approximately 12 months ago, prior to the election of Councilmember Sipes. 
The city council makes employment decisions regarding the city manager. These include 
decisions to continue the city manager's employment, evaluate her performance and set her 
salary. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their' 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the 
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 8100l(b).) Section 87100 
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. 


File No. A-11-007 
Page No. 3 
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an 
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, 
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental 
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her 
financial interests. 
Step 1. Is Councilmember Sipes a "public official" within the meaning of Section 87100? 
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or consultant 
of a state or local government agency." As a member of the city council, which is a local 
government agency, Councilmember Sipes is a public official. Therefore, he may not make, 
participate in making, or otherwise use his position to make, participate in making or influence 
any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of his 
economic interests. 
Step 2. Will Councilmember Sipes be making, participating in making or influencing a 
governmental decision? 
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the 
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her 
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her 
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, 
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or 
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the 
decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is 
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if, for the 
purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts 
to influence, any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 
18702.3.) 
Councilmember Sipes will be making and participating in city council decisions if the 
city council votes to expand or eliminate the Program or set eligibility criteria for the Program. 
Also, he will be making and participating in city council decisions regarding the continued 
employment, performance evaluations and salary of the city manager. Please note, when a 
public official holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision 
noticed at a public meeting. he or she must (1) immediately prior to the decision of the item, 
orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision, as well as details of the 
economic interest, as discussed in Regulation 18702.S(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) 
recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room•for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on 
the item. For closed session, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the 
public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.5(c) and 18702.S(d) 
apply. 


Step 3. What are Councilmember Sipes' economic interests? 
File No. A-11-007 
Page No. 4 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising 
from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section 
87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive: 
• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he 
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 
18703.l (a).) 
• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or 
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.l (b).) 
• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she 
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.) 
• An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including 
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) 
• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her 
if the gifts total $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); 
Regulation 18703.4.) 
• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, 
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is commonly 
referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) 
Business Entity (the Mortgage Company and the Real Estate Partnership) 
Councilmember Sipes has an economic interest in the Mortgage Company and the Real 
Estate Partnership because he is a partner in each of these business entities. (Section 87103(d); 
Regulation 18703(b).) In addition, he would have an economic interest in these business entities 
if his investment in each is worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703. l (a).) 
Source of /11come (the Mortgage Company, its clients, the Real Estcue Partnership and its 
I 
renters.) 
The Mortgage Company is a source of income to Councilmember Sipes because, 
presumably, he receives annual income of over $500 from the Mortgage Company. Also, if he 
receives annual income of over $500 from the Real Estate Partnership, the partnership would be 
a source of income economic to him. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) 


File No. A-11-007 
Page No. 5 
ln addition, assuming Councilmember Sipes owns an interest of ten percent or greater in 
each of these business entities, any source of income of the business entity will be a source of 
income to him. (Section 82030(a).) For the Mortgage Company, this would include clients that 
apply to the Program. For the Real Estate Partnership, this would include the city manager. 
Real Property 
You state that Councilmember Sipes' name and the names of his three partners are on the 
title of the real property leased to the city manager by the Real Estate Partnership. As an owner 
of the property, Councilmember Sipes has an economic interest in the real property if his interest 
is worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)2 
Personal Finances 
Councilmember Sipes would have an economic interest in a governmental decision if the 
decision would affect his personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities, as well as those of his 
immediate family. (Regulation 18705.5.) You have not indicated any other economic interests. 
Accordingly, our analysis is limited to the above identified economic interests. 
Step 4. Will Councilmember Sipes' economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in 
decisions he will make, participate in making or influence as a public official? 
Business Entities and Sources of Income: 
Under Regulation 18704. l(a) a person, including business entities and sources of income, 
is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or 
by an agent: 
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing 
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; 
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the 
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the 
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, 
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement 
to, or contract with, the subject person." 
If a public official's economic interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is 
indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704(a).) 
2 Please note that an "interest in real property" of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real 
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns. directly or indirectly, or 
beneficially, a I 0-percent interest or greater (Section 82033.) As a 25% owner of the Real Estate Partnership. he has 
a pro rata ownership interest in all the property of the partnership. 


File No. A-11-007 
Page No. 6 
Mortgage Company and Clients/Applicants 
It does not appear that the Mortgage Company or clients/applicants will initiate or be 
parties in or the subject of a proceeding involving the expansion, elimination or the setting of 
eligibility criteria for the Program. Accordingly, the Mortgage Company and Councilmember 
Sipes' clients would be indirectly involved in these governmental decisions. 
With respect to individual applications for the Program, please note that even if the city 
council does not make, or participate in making, decisions to approve the applications, 
Councilmember Sipes may have a disqualifying conflict of interest if he uses his official position 
to influence a decision by staff or the Finance Committee regarding an application by a 
Mortgage Company client. In that case, the client would be directly involved in the decision. 
We simply point this out for infonnational purposes because your facts do not suggest this is the 
case. 
Real Estate Partnership and City Manager 
It does not appear that the real estate partnership will initiate or be a named party in or the 
subject of any proceeding involving the city manager. Accordingly, the real estate partnership is 
deemed to be indirectly involved. 
The city manager is directly involved in city council decisions regarding her employment 
because she is a named party in and the subject of proceedings concerning her employment. 
(Regulation 18704.l(a)(2).) 
Real Property 
Under Section 18704.2(a), real property is directly involved in a governmental decision 
if: 
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any 
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the 
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is 
the subject of the governmental decision. For purposes of 
subdivision (a)(5), real property is located within 500 feet of the 
boundaries ( or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is 
the subject of the governmental decision if any part of the real 
property is within 500 feet of the boundaries ( or proposed 
boundaries) of the redevelopment project area. 
"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning, 
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion 
in or exclusion from any city, county, district or other local 
governmental subdivision, of the real property in which the official 


File No. A-11-007 
Page No. 7 
has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property. For 
purposes of this subdivision, the tenns 'zoning' and 'rezoning' 
shall refer to the act of establishing or changing the zoning or land 
use designation on the real property in which the official has an 
interest. 
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or 
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement 
authorizing a specific use or uses of the real property in which the 
official has an interest. 
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or 
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real 
property in which the official has an interest. 
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to 
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a 
project area committee, to certify the environmental document, to 
adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the 
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above 
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or 
any part of it is located within the boundaries (or the proposed 
boundaries) of the redevelopment area. 
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, 
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the 
real property in which the official has an interest will receive new 
or improved services." 
Because the governmental decisions coming before Councilmember Sipes do not fall within any 
of the foregoing categories, Councilmember Sipes' real property is deemed to be indirectly 
involved. 
Personal Finances 
A public official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be directly involved in a 
governmental decision that has any financial effect on the public official's personal expenses, 
income, assets or liabilities or those of the official's immediate family. 
Step 5. What'is the' applicable materiality standard? 
Business Entity (the Mortgage Company and the Real Estate Partnership) 
The applicable materiality standards for governmental decisions for business entities 
indirectly involved in a governmental decision are set forth in Regulation 18705.l(c). For 


File No. A-11-007 
Page No. 8 
relatively small businesses (those with annual net income of $750,000 or less) the financial effect 
of a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the governmental decision will increase/decrease the business entity's annual gross revenues by 
$20,000 or more, result in the business entity incurring/avoiding additional expenses or 
reducing/eliminating existing expenses by $5,000 or more in a fiscal year or 
increasing/decreasing the value of the business entity's assets by $20,000 or more. (Regulation 
18705. l(c)(4).) 
Source of Income (the Mortgage Company, its clients, the Real Estate Partnership and the city 
manager) 
The applicable materiality standards for persons, including individuals and business 
entities, that are sources of income to a public official are set forth in Regulation 18705.3. As 
stated above, the Mortgage Company, its clients and the Real Estate Partnership are indirectly 
involved in the governmental decisions you have identified. For sources of income that are 
indirectly involved, the applicable materiality standard depends on whether the source of income 
is an individual or a business entity. For sources of income that are business entities (e.g. the 
Mortgage Company and the Real Estate Partnership), the materiality standards for business 
entities, discussed above, apply. For sources of income that are individuals (e.g. the city 
manager), the financial effect of a decision is material if it will affect the individual's income, 
investments, or other assets or liabilities ( other than real property) by $1,000 or more. 
However, the city manager would be directly involved in decisions affecting her 
employment. "Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income 
to a public official and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is 
deemed material." (Regulation 18705.3(a), emphasis added.) 
Real Property 
The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property that is indirectly 
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be 
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its 
financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic 
interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on the real property. From the facts you have provided, it does not appear that there are 
circumstances that would rebut the presumption. Accordingly, the financial effect of the 
governmental decisions on Councilmember Sipes• real property is presumed not to be material. 
Personal Finances 
A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Councilmember Sipes' or his immediate 
family's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period. (Regulation 
18705.5.) When determining whether a governmental decision has a material financial effect on 
a public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a financial effect on 
the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the 


File No. A-11-007 
Page No. 9 
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets or liabilities of a business entity in which the official 
has a direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered. (Regulation 18705.S(a).) You 
have not described any effects on the councilmember's personal finances other than effects on 
his business and real property, therefore we do not further analyze this economic interest. 
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effects of a governmental 
decision on Councilmember Sipes' economic interests will meet the applicable materiality 
standard? 
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a 
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. A financial effect 
need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is 
only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. See Regulation 18706, a copy of which 
is enclosed, for factors that should be considered in determining whether a financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable. 
With respect to city council decisions involving the Program, Councilmember Sipes will 
need to determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect (1) his company's 
annual gross revenues or assets by $20,000 or expenses by $5,000, or (2) the income, 
investments, or other assets or liabilities (other than real property) of any of the Mortgage 
Company's client's by $1,000. 
For decisions involving the city manager, it appears that decisions regarding her 
continued employment, performance evaluations and salary are likely to affect her income and 
expenses. Because this is the case, Councilmember Sipes may not participate in these decisions. 
Steps 7 and 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation" 
exceptions. 
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably 
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental 
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the 
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)), or if the official is 
legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708). These exceptions are complex 
and we recommend that Councilmember Sipes requests advice if he believes that an exception 
applies to a particular situation. 
In summary, when Councilmember Sipes is faced with one of the governmental decisions 
identified in your letter, he must analyze each decision, applying the rules described above, to 
determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 'financial effect on 
any of his economic interests. 


VJ:jgl 
File No. A-11-007 
Page No. 10 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Scott Hallabrin 
General Counsel 
J 
By: Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
May Councilmember Sipes participate in decisions involving: 
1. the expansion or elimination of or the setting of eligibility criteria for the city's First 
Time Buyer Program (the "Program") if clients of a business in which he is a partner apply for 
the Program, or 
2. the salary, performance evaluations or continued employment of the city manager who 
leases property from a partnership in which Councilmember Sipes is a partner?
sections.conclusion:
l. Councilmember Sipes may participate in a decision regarding the Program unless it 
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect (i) his company's annual gross revenues or 
assets by $20,000 or expenses by $5,000, or (ii) the income, investments, or other assets or 
liabilities (other than real property) of any of the company's client's by $1,000.2. Councilmember Sipes may not participate in a decision regarding the city manager's 
employment because it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on the city manager's income.
sections.facts:
Brian Sipes was recently elected to the city council for the City of Fillmore. He, together 
with his mother, is a partner in a local mortgage lending company (the "Mortgage Company"). 
Prior to his election, Councilmember Sipes and his Mortgage Company have arranged loans for 
home buyers. Some of his clients have applied for the city's Program. The Program provides a

not a member of the Finance Committee and has no authority to appoint members to the Finance 
Committee. 
Once a client of the Mortgage Company has submitted an application, the Mortgage 
Company follows up on the processing of the application. The Mortgage Company does not fill 
out the application for its clients. The Mortgage Company does not receive any payment from 
proceeds of the loan. 
Applications for the Program do not come before the city council. However, the city 
council has the power to vote to expand or eliminate the Program or to set eligibility criteria for 
the Program. There are no current plans to do so. 
Councilmember Sipes is also a partner in a partnership with three other individuals that 
own properties within the city limits (the "Real Estate Partnership.") One of the properties is

approximately 12 months ago, prior to the election of Councilmember Sipes. 
The city council makes employment decisions regarding the city manager. These include 
decisions to continue the city manager's employment, evaluate her performance and set her 
salary.
sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their' 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the 
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 8100l(b).) Section 87100 
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest.  
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an 
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, 
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental 
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her 
financial interests. 
Step 1. Is Councilmember Sipes a "public official" within the meaning of Section 87100? 
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or consultant 
of a state or local government agency." As a member of the city council, which is a local 
government agency, Councilmember Sipes is a public official. Therefore, he may not make, 
participate in making, or otherwise use his position to make, participate in making or influence 
any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of his 
economic interests. 
Step 2. Will Councilmember Sipes be making, participating in making or influencing a 
governmental decision? 
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the 
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her 
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her 
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, 
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or 
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the 
decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is 
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if, for the 
purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts 
to influence, any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 
18702.3.) 
Councilmember Sipes will be making and participating in city council decisions if the 
city council votes to expand or eliminate the Program or set eligibility criteria for the Program. 
Also, he will be making and participating in city council decisions regarding the continued 
employment, performance evaluations and salary of the city manager. Please note, when a 
public official holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision 
noticed at a public meeting. he or she must (1) immediately prior to the decision of the item, 
orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision, as well as details of the 
economic interest, as discussed in Regulation 18702.S(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) 
recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room•for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on 
the item. For closed session, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the 
public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.5(c) and 18702.S(d) 
apply. 


Step 3. What are Councilmember Sipes' economic interests?  
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising 
from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section 
87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive: 
• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he 
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 
18703.l (a).) 
• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or 
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.l (b).) 
• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she 
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.) 
• An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including 
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) 
• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her 
if the gifts total $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); 
Regulation 18703.4.) 
• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, 
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is commonly 
referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) 
Business Entity (the Mortgage Company and the Real Estate Partnership) 
Councilmember Sipes has an economic interest in the Mortgage Company and the Real 
Estate Partnership because he is a partner in each of these business entities. (Section 87103(d); 
Regulation 18703(b).) In addition, he would have an economic interest in these business entities 
if his investment in each is worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703. l (a).) 
Source of /11come (the Mortgage Company, its clients, the Real Estcue Partnership and its 
I 
renters.) 
The Mortgage Company is a source of income to Councilmember Sipes because, 
presumably, he receives annual income of over $500 from the Mortgage Company. Also, if he 
receives annual income of over $500 from the Real Estate Partnership, the partnership would be 
a source of income economic to him. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)  
ln addition, assuming Councilmember Sipes owns an interest of ten percent or greater in 
each of these business entities, any source of income of the business entity will be a source of 
income to him. (Section 82030(a).) For the Mortgage Company, this would include clients that 
apply to the Program. For the Real Estate Partnership, this would include the city manager. 
Real Property 
You state that Councilmember Sipes' name and the names of his three partners are on the 
title of the real property leased to the city manager by the Real Estate Partnership. As an owner 
of the property, Councilmember Sipes has an economic interest in the real property if his interest 
is worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)2 
Personal Finances 
Councilmember Sipes would have an economic interest in a governmental decision if the 
decision would affect his personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities, as well as those of his 
immediate family. (Regulation 18705.5.) You have not indicated any other economic interests. 
Accordingly, our analysis is limited to the above identified economic interests. 
Step 4. Will Councilmember Sipes' economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in 
decisions he will make, participate in making or influence as a public official? 
Business Entities and Sources of Income: 
Under Regulation 18704. l(a) a person, including business entities and sources of income, 
is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or 
by an agent: 
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing 
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; 
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the 
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the 
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, 
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement 
to, or contract with, the subject person." 
If a public official's economic interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is 
indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704(a).) 
2 Please note that an "interest in real property" of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real 
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns. directly or indirectly, or 
beneficially, a I 0-percent interest or greater (Section 82033.) As a 25% owner of the Real Estate Partnership. he has 
a pro rata ownership interest in all the property of the partnership.  
Mortgage Company and Clients/Applicants 
It does not appear that the Mortgage Company or clients/applicants will initiate or be 
parties in or the subject of a proceeding involving the expansion, elimination or the setting of 
eligibility criteria for the Program. Accordingly, the Mortgage Company and Councilmember 
Sipes' clients would be indirectly involved in these governmental decisions. 
With respect to individual applications for the Program, please note that even if the city 
council does not make, or participate in making, decisions to approve the applications, 
Councilmember Sipes may have a disqualifying conflict of interest if he uses his official position 
to influence a decision by staff or the Finance Committee regarding an application by a 
Mortgage Company client. In that case, the client would be directly involved in the decision. 
We simply point this out for infonnational purposes because your facts do not suggest this is the 
case. 
Real Estate Partnership and City Manager 
It does not appear that the real estate partnership will initiate or be a named party in or the 
subject of any proceeding involving the city manager. Accordingly, the real estate partnership is 
deemed to be indirectly involved. 
The city manager is directly involved in city council decisions regarding her employment 
because she is a named party in and the subject of proceedings concerning her employment. 
(Regulation 18704.l(a)(2).) 
Real Property 
Under Section 18704.2(a), real property is directly involved in a governmental decision 
if: 
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any 
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the 
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is 
the subject of the governmental decision. For purposes of 
subdivision (a)(5), real property is located within 500 feet of the 
boundaries ( or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is 
the subject of the governmental decision if any part of the real 
property is within 500 feet of the boundaries ( or proposed 
boundaries) of the redevelopment project area. 
"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning, 
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion 
in or exclusion from any city, county, district or other local 
governmental subdivision, of the real property in which the official  
has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property. For 
purposes of this subdivision, the tenns 'zoning' and 'rezoning' 
shall refer to the act of establishing or changing the zoning or land 
use designation on the real property in which the official has an 
interest. 
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or 
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement 
authorizing a specific use or uses of the real property in which the 
official has an interest. 
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or 
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real 
property in which the official has an interest. 
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to 
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a 
project area committee, to certify the environmental document, to 
adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the 
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above 
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or 
any part of it is located within the boundaries (or the proposed 
boundaries) of the redevelopment area. 
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, 
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the 
real property in which the official has an interest will receive new 
or improved services." 
Because the governmental decisions coming before Councilmember Sipes do not fall within any 
of the foregoing categories, Councilmember Sipes' real property is deemed to be indirectly 
involved. 
Personal Finances 
A public official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be directly involved in a 
governmental decision that has any financial effect on the public official's personal expenses, 
income, assets or liabilities or those of the official's immediate family. 
Step 5. What'is the' applicable materiality standard? 
Business Entity (the Mortgage Company and the Real Estate Partnership) 
The applicable materiality standards for governmental decisions for business entities 
indirectly involved in a governmental decision are set forth in Regulation 18705.l(c). For  
relatively small businesses (those with annual net income of $750,000 or less) the financial effect 
of a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the governmental decision will increase/decrease the business entity's annual gross revenues by 
$20,000 or more, result in the business entity incurring/avoiding additional expenses or 
reducing/eliminating existing expenses by $5,000 or more in a fiscal year or 
increasing/decreasing the value of the business entity's assets by $20,000 or more. (Regulation 
18705. l(c)(4).) 
Source of Income (the Mortgage Company, its clients, the Real Estate Partnership and the city 
manager) 
The applicable materiality standards for persons, including individuals and business 
entities, that are sources of income to a public official are set forth in Regulation 18705.3. As 
stated above, the Mortgage Company, its clients and the Real Estate Partnership are indirectly 
involved in the governmental decisions you have identified. For sources of income that are 
indirectly involved, the applicable materiality standard depends on whether the source of income 
is an individual or a business entity. For sources of income that are business entities (e.g. the 
Mortgage Company and the Real Estate Partnership), the materiality standards for business 
entities, discussed above, apply. For sources of income that are individuals (e.g. the city 
manager), the financial effect of a decision is material if it will affect the individual's income, 
investments, or other assets or liabilities ( other than real property) by $1,000 or more. 
However, the city manager would be directly involved in decisions affecting her 
employment. "Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income 
to a public official and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is 
deemed material." (Regulation 18705.3(a), emphasis added.) 
Real Property 
The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property that is indirectly 
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be 
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its 
financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic 
interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on the real property. From the facts you have provided, it does not appear that there are 
circumstances that would rebut the presumption. Accordingly, the financial effect of the 
governmental decisions on Councilmember Sipes• real property is presumed not to be material. 
Personal Finances 
A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Councilmember Sipes' or his immediate 
family's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period. (Regulation 
18705.5.) When determining whether a governmental decision has a material financial effect on 
a public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a financial effect on 
the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the  
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets or liabilities of a business entity in which the official 
has a direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered. (Regulation 18705.S(a).) You 
have not described any effects on the councilmember's personal finances other than effects on 
his business and real property, therefore we do not further analyze this economic interest. 
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effects of a governmental 
decision on Councilmember Sipes' economic interests will meet the applicable materiality 
standard? 
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a 
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. A financial effect 
need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is 
only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. See Regulation 18706, a copy of which 
is enclosed, for factors that should be considered in determining whether a financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable. 
With respect to city council decisions involving the Program, Councilmember Sipes will 
need to determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect (1) his company's 
annual gross revenues or assets by $20,000 or expenses by $5,000, or (2) the income, 
investments, or other assets or liabilities (other than real property) of any of the Mortgage 
Company's client's by $1,000. 
For decisions involving the city manager, it appears that decisions regarding her 
continued employment, performance evaluations and salary are likely to affect her income and 
expenses. Because this is the case, Councilmember Sipes may not participate in these decisions. 
Steps 7 and 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation" 
exceptions. 
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably 
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental 
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the 
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)), or if the official is 
legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708). These exceptions are complex 
and we recommend that Councilmember Sipes requests advice if he believes that an exception 
applies to a particular situation. 
In summary, when Councilmember Sipes is faced with one of the governmental decisions 
identified in your letter, he must analyze each decision, applying the rules described above, to 
determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 'financial effect on 
any of his economic interests.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Sipes participate in decisions involving: 
1. the expansion or elimination of or the setting of eligibility criteria for the city's First 
Time Buyer Program (the "Program") if clients of a business in which he is a partner apply for 
the Program, or 
2. the salary, performance evaluations or continued employment of the city manager who 
leases property from a partnership in which Councilmember Sipes is a partner?

CONCLUSION: l. Councilmember Sipes may participate in a decision regarding the Program unless it 
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect (i) his company's annual gross revenues or 
assets by $20,000 or expenses by $5,000, or (ii) the income, investments, or other assets or 
liabilities (other than real property) of any of the company's client's by $1,000.2. Councilmember Sipes may not participate in a decision regarding the city manager's 
employment because it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on the city manager's income.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 Patti Walker Mayor, City of Fillmore Central Park Plaza 250 Central A venue Fillmore, California 93015-1907 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-11-007 Dear Ms. Walker: (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 April 18, 2011 This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. You are writing on behalf of Councilmember Brian Sipes. QUESTIONS May Councilmember Sipes participate in decisions involving: 1. the expansion or elimination of or the setting of eligibility criteria for the city's First Time Buyer Program (the "Program") if clients of a business in which he is a partner apply for the Program, or 2. the salary, performance evaluations or continued employment of the city manager who leases property from a partnership in which Councilmember Sipes is a partner? CONCLUSIONS l. Councilmember Sipes may participate in a decision regarding the Program unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect (i) his company's annual gross revenues or assets by $20,000 or expenses by $5,000, or (ii) the income, investments, or other assets or liabilities (other than real property) of any of the company's client's by $1,000. 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9 IO 14. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 181 IO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-11-007 Page No. 2 2. Councilmember Sipes may not participate in a decision regarding the city manager's employment because it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the city manager's income. FACTS Brian Sipes was recently elected to the city council for the City of Fillmore. He, together with his mother, is a partner in a local mortgage lending company (the "Mortgage Company"). Prior to his election, Councilmember Sipes and his Mortgage Company have arranged loans for home buyers. Some of his clients have applied for the city's Program. The Program provides a loan of up to $50,000 for qualified first time home buyers to be used for a down payment and closing costs. A borrower's application is reviewed by staff and then presented to the Finance Committee for approval. The Finance Committee has two members. Councilmember Sipes is not a member of the Finance Committee and has no authority to appoint members to the Finance Committee. Once a client of the Mortgage Company has submitted an application, the Mortgage Company follows up
